model.label: Kimi K2.5
model.description: Moonshot AI • Reasoning model from the Kimi family built for complex thinking tasks.

pick.rationale: Michigan holds a commanding statistical advantage as the #2 ranked team nationally with a historic +38.38 adjusted efficiency margin that exceeds Virginia's +26.49 mark by nearly 12 points. The Wolverines' elite #2 ranked defense presents a severe mismatch against Virginia's 14th-ranked unit, while Michigan's top-8 offense faces a favorable matchup against the Cavaliers' weaker attack. Although Virginia enters at full strength with ACC championship pedigree after dispatching Kentucky, the 11-12 spot ranking gap and massive efficiency differential favor Michigan decisively in this 1/3 matchup. Yaxel Lendeborg's ankle sprain appears manageable after playing in the opening rounds, preserving Michigan's championship-caliber two-way excellence that has carried them to the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: Michigan vs Virginia Elite 8 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Comprehensive analysis of Midwest Regional Final shows Michigan holds overwhelming statistical advantages in efficiency margin (+38.38 vs +26.49), national ranking (#2 vs #13-14), and defensive profile (#2 vs #14). Virginia's 29-4 record and ACC championship demonstrate quality but face a prohibitive mismatch against one of the nation's most dominant teams.
evidence: Michigan: KenPom #2, Torvik #2, 31-2 record, +38.38 adj EM, #2 defense, #7-8 offense
Virginia: KenPom #13, Torvik #14, 29-4 record, +26.49 adj EM, #14 defense, #28-31 offense
Injury status: Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg (ankle) played Round 1, expected to continue; Virginia at full strength
Historical pattern: 1-seeds vs 3-seeds in Elite Eight favor higher seed ~65-70%, widening with 10+ point efficiency margins

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

1. **Michigan** (slotA, michigan):
   - KenPom/Torvik Rank: #2 nationally
   - Record: 31-2
   - Adj. Efficiency Margin: +38.38 (historic)
   - Offense: #7-8 nationally
   - Defense: #2 nationally
   - Has Yaxel Lendeborg with a minor ankle sprain, but he played in Round 1 and expects to continue
   - 1-seed in Midwest Region

2. **Virginia** (slotB, virginia):
   - KenPom/Torvik Rank: #13-14 nationally
   - Record: 29-4
   - Adj. Efficiency Margin: +26.49
   - Offense: #28-31 nationally
   - Defense: #14 nationally
   - ACC champions, 3-seed
   - Full strength

The statistical gap is enormous - Michigan has a nearly 12-point efficiency margin advantage (+38.38 vs +26.49), ranks 11-12 spots higher, and possesses both a top-8 offense and the #2 defense nationally. Virginia has a good defense (#14) but not on Michigan's level, and their offense is significantly weaker.

Looking at prior picks context:
- Michigan beat Alabama (4-seed) in Sweet 16 with 84% confidence
- Virginia beat Kentucky (7-seed) in Sweet 16 with 72% confidence

Michigan is a dominant 1-seed with historic efficiency metrics. Virginia is a strong 3-seed but faces a massive statistical disadvantage against one of the best teams in the country.

I should pick Michigan with high confidence, likely in the 80-85% range. The rationale should highlight the efficiency margin gap, defensive superiority, and Michigan's balanced attack.

Based on my analysis, I have comprehensive data to make this pick.

  "pick": {

    "confidence": 0.86,
    "rationale": "Michigan holds a commanding statistical advantage as the #2 ranked team nationally with a historic +38.38 adjusted efficiency margin that exceeds Virginia's +26.49 mark by nearly 12 points. The Wolverines' elite #2 ranked defense presents a severe mismatch against Virginia's 14th-ranked unit, while Michigan's top-8 offense faces a favorable matchup against the Cavaliers' weaker attack. Although Virginia enters at full strength with ACC championship pedigree after dispatching Kentucky, the 11-12 spot ranking gap and massive efficiency differential favor Michigan decisively in this 1/3 matchup. Yaxel Lendeborg's ankle sprain appears manageable after playing in the opening rounds, preserving Michigan's championship-caliber two-way excellence that has carried them to the Elite Eight."
  },
  "reasoningStep": {

    "summary": "Comprehensive analysis of Midwest Regional Final shows Michigan holds overwhelming statistical advantages in efficiency margin (+38.38 vs +26.49), national ranking (#2 vs #13-14), and defensive profile (#2 vs #14). Virginia's 29-4 record and ACC championship demonstrate quality but face a prohibitive mismatch against one of the nation's most dominant teams.",
    "evidence": {

      },

        "record": "29-4",
        "adj_em": 26.49,
        "offense_rank": "28-31",

      },
